original_hypothesis.title: BlackholePairAPIV2.initialize allows setting arbitrary external dependencies; misconfiguration could make API return misleading routing/pricing and break downstream automation
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: BlackholePairAPIV2 stores critical dependency addresses (voter, routerV2, gaugeManager, pairFactory, algebraFactory, quoterV2, algebraPoolAPIStorage) from initialize(). If deployed behind a proxy, any incorrect initialization parameters (or later admin changes via setVoter/setGaugeManager/setPairFactory/setQuoterV2/setAlgebraFactory/setAlgebraPoolAPI) can point the API to malicious/incorrect contracts. Downstream bots/frontends relying on this API could route trades incorrectly or display false values.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While primarily an admin/config risk, this contract is intended as a canonical data API. Misconfiguration is practical and can lead to systemic user harm. Validate access control on setters and whether this API is treated as trusted by other protocol components.
deployment_assumptions: API contract is treated as an authoritative source by integrations or automation
Owner key is compromised OR owner acts maliciously OR deployment initialized with wrong addresses
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Deployment script deploy-pair-api.js shows proxy deployment with initializer parameters; setters exist with only `require(msg.sender==owner)` using a custom owner variable (not OZ Ownable).

inventory_refs.function: initialize(address _voter, address _router, address _gaugeManager, address _pairFactory, address _algebraFactory, address _quoterV2, address _algebraPoolAPIStorage)

proof_of_signal.pattern: initializer sets multiple dependency addresses; later setters gated only by custom `owner` variable; not OZ Ownable

preconditions: Owner can call setters (key compromise/malicious admin) OR initializer parameters are wrong
Downstream systems trust this API for routing/pricing decisions

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: centralized mutable dependency injection without strong governance controls
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: API can be re-pointed to malicious router/quoter/factory returning arbitrary outputs
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No timelock; no events for all config changes (some have events, but not for all setters); custom owner pattern

analysis_questions: Is this API called by any on-chain contracts (must not trust it)?
Are setter calls monitored / restricted operationally?
Is there any proxy admin that can also upgrade logic?
validation_requirements: Trace integration points: frontend, bots, keepers
Review role management and key custody for API owner
Verify events emitted on each config change for monitoring

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker with owner key points routerV2/quoterV2 to malicious contracts that return manipulated route outputs, causing users to execute poor trades or bots to behave incorrectly.
prerequisites: Off-chain compromise or malicious admin (owner key)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect but potentially widespread user loss via misrouting/mispricing by trusted integrations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users relying on the API for quotes/routes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium; depends on detection and reverting config

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests asserting API is not used as an on-chain oracle and that config changes are observable

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: event-based monitoring for API dependency changes

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
